verses.commentary: Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. Justification produces peace. Not peace with ourselves or circumstances, but peace with God. The war is over; hostility has ended. Jesus Christ is the mediator of this peace.

Through whom we have gained access by faith into this grace in which we now stand. Access (prosagōgē, προσαγωγή) means introduction, like entering the king's presence. We stand in grace, not by our merit. And we boast in the hope of the glory of God. Hope is confident expectation. We will share in God's glory, the very thing we fell short of (3:23).

Not only so, but we also glory in our sufferings, because we know that suffering produces perseverance. Rejoicing in suffering seems paradoxical. But suffering is not meaningless; it produces character. Perseverance (hypomonē, ὑπομονή) is endurance under trial.

Perseverance, character; and character, hope. A progression: suffering leads to perseverance, which forms character (dokimē, δοκιμή, proven quality), which strengthens hope. Trials refine and confirm faith.

And hope does not put us to shame, because God's love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, who has been given to us. Hope is certain, not wishful thinking. The ground of hope is God's love poured out (perfect tense: already given and continuing) into our hearts by the Holy Spirit. The Spirit is the guarantee of future glory.

You see, at just the right time, when we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly. God's timing is perfect. We were powerless (asthenēs, ἀσθενής, weak, unable). Christ died not for the righteous but for the ungodly. This is the scandal of grace.

Very rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person someone might possibly dare to die. Human love has limits. Dying for a righteous or good person is rare. But Christ's love exceeds human love.

But God demonstrates His own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us. God's love is demonstrated, not just declared. While we were still sinners, not after we cleaned up. This is love for enemies, the undeserving. The cross is the proof of God's love.

Since we have now been justified by His blood, how much more shall we be saved from God's wrath through Him! If God reconciled us while we were enemies, how much more will He save us now that we are justified! The logic is from the greater to the lesser. Justification guarantees final salvation.

For if, while we were God's enemies, we were reconciled to Him through the death of His Son, how much more, having been reconciled, shall we be saved through His life! Reconciliation happened when we were enemies. Now that we are reconciled, salvation is certain. Christ's resurrection life secures our salvation.

Not only is this so, but we also boast in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation. Boasting shifts from self to God. Reconciliation (katallagē, καταλλαγή) is the restoration of relationship. We rejoice not in ourselves but in God.

Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned. Paul introduces the Adam-Christ parallel. Sin entered through Adam; death followed sin. Death spread to all because all sinned. Original sin: Adam's sin affects all his descendants.

To be sure, sin was in the world before the law was given, but sin is not charged against anyone's account where there is no law. Sin existed before Sinai, but without law, sin is not formally counted as transgression. This does not mean sin was not real, but it was not legally charged.

Nevertheless, death reigned from the time of Adam to the time of Moses, even over those who did not sin by breaking a command, as did Adam, who is a pattern of the one to come. Death proves universal sin. Even those who did not break an explicit command like Adam still died. Adam is a type (typos, τύπος) of Christ: one man's act affects many.

But the gift is not like the trespass. For if the many died by the trespass of the one man, how much more did God's grace and the gift that came by the grace of the one man, Jesus Christ, overflow to the many! The contrast: Adam brought death; Christ brought life. But Christ's work exceeds Adam's in power and scope. Grace overflows beyond the damage of sin.

Nor can the gift of God be compared with the result of one man's sin: The judgment followed one sin and brought condemnation, but the gift followed many trespasses and brought justification. One sin (Adam's) brought condemnation. But grace covers many sins and brings justification. Grace is greater than sin.

For if, by the trespass of the one man, death reigned through that one man, how much more will those who receive God's abundant provision of grace and of the gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man, Jesus Christ! Death reigned through Adam. But those in Christ will reign in life. Not just escape death, but reign. Grace reverses the curse and elevates believers beyond Adam's original state.

Consequently, just as one trespass resulted in condemnation for all people, so also one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people. The parallel is complete. Adam's one sin brought condemnation to all. Christ's one righteous act (His death and resurrection) brings justification and life to all who believe. All people means all in Adam or all in Christ, not universal salvation.

For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous. Adam's disobedience constituted many as sinners. Christ's obedience constitutes many as righteous. Imputation works both ways: Adam's sin imputed to us, Christ's righteousness imputed to us.

The law was brought in so that the trespass might increase. But where sin increased, grace increased all the more. The law's purpose was to expose and multiply awareness of sin. But grace is greater than sin. Sin abounded, but grace superabounded (hyperperisseuō, ὑπερπερισσεύω). Grace always exceeds sin.

So that, just as sin reigned in death, so also grace might reign through righteousness to bring eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord. Two kingdoms: sin's reign brings death; grace's reign brings life. Grace reigns through righteousness (not despite it). The ground is Christ's work. The result is eternal life.
